speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Bruce Cohen, and I'm interviewing Halina Nelken. The date is 24 November, 1995, and this is Cambridge, Massachusetts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape is  rolling. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'd like to thank you very much for welcoming us so graciously into your home. And we're very, very proud to be interviewing you this afternoon. Could you please state and spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Halina Nelken, H-A-L-I-N-A N-E-L-K-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have you ever been known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, shortly, or shortly-- for three years or so-- by my married name, Morawska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-O-R-A-W-S-K-A. But I never get rid of Nelken. And even my first book was published under Halina Nelken Morawska. I dropped that name when I become American citizen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Your birth date is?

speaker: interviewee
text: September 20, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that means today you are how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, don't even say. Yes, today I am--

speaker: interviewer
text: For archival purposes, you are how old today?

speaker: interviewer
text: 70. And the city and country of your birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Krakow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: All  right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To begin--

speaker: interviewee
text: Did you ever met any 70-years-old woman who is blushing?

speaker: interviewer
text: All the time. Well, let's begin with-- with your life in-- in Poland. Could you tell us or describe your family, your most immediate family, starting with your mother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came from a family who belonged to a slowly disappearing social strata called intelligentsia. Well, my family, since grandparent and since many, many years, were assimilated Poles. We always were Poles of [? Mosaic ?] faith. We are first Poles, then we were Jewish at the same time. My grandfather, my father, my brother, they all participated in that fight for Polish independence. My great-grandfather was at the uprising against Russia in 1863. My father was with Pilsudski's Legion. He was a legionnaire during the First World War. My brother, while he was 18, he was from the School of Cadets fighting in the Second World War defending Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was this kind of a family of that so-called working intelligentsia. My father was a consultant and working in the National Bank of Poland. My mother was very much interested in history. And they were just doing some kind of charities with-- But rather, whenever-- when I came to America, everybody ask, how come you don't have family here? And I knew that I don't because our family did not emigrate. They were in the mainstream of Polish social, cultural, and-- and even political life. And just to backtrack slightly, could you give us the name of your father and your  mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Edmund Emanuel Nelken. My mother was Regina Barber.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell both names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edmund is E-D-M-U-N-D. Emanuel is E-M-A-N-U-E-L, Nelken. [BACKGROUND NOISE] [POINTS] My-- my-- I'm sorry. We have a neighbor on the second floor, higher floor--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --who is playing. Well, and as a matter of fact, my mother was Regina Barber, B-A-R-B-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And when was your father born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember very well, but I think that he was born on 12 of April, 19-- 1890 or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he lived until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lived until May 14 or 15, 1944, when he was sent to gas chamber [INAUDIBLE] and transport of Hungarian to which the transport of men from Plaszow was with it-- together. And I found his file in central files in Auschwitz, and, therefore, I know when he was sent to gas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 14 or 15 May 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your mother was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was born in Krakow on, I think, 7th of September, 1990-- 9-- 9-- 9-- I'm sorry, 18-- [LAUGHS] 1890, or 18-- 9-- something like this, '95. Well, I don't remember exactly. She die here in this country on 19th of March, 1963.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a result of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sudden heart attack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, by occupation, she was what in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was historian, first of all. But she was doing different things. In particular during the war, everybody had thousands of professions. But my mother knew how to do accounting and this, and she was doing this for that orphanage in the ghetto for the Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that called?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just very briefly, your father's primary interests?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents had so many interests. First of all, music. Everybody was playing in the family or singing. Then father was very much interested in the symphony orchestra in Krakow. And as a matter of fact, a performer, Alexander Weissenberg, who is a great pianist even here, when he was a small child, my father organized a concert for him in Krakow. Also the sport, of course. And then theater, opera. Well, one could hardly say they were hobbies. They were the interest of everybody of this-- this intelligentsia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your-- your father was originally trained where?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was trained in Vienna, Handel's Academy. Well, in-- yes. And, of course, books. We had a very large library, and part of this library we took with us to ghetto. And there was where I was able to read all these books that, under normal circumstances, I wasn't supposed to read at this age. But I read all that French literature. We have in translation of and-- and, therefore, all this and all these kind of things I was reading. Hardly even knowing what I'm reading, but I did. [LAUGHS] And, of course, all the German main poets, Goethe, Schiller, Heine, and-- and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: --primary interests? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had the same interests. And of course, we all love dogs. We always have animals around the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother's name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name was Feliks, F-E-L-I-K-S. And here Felix there on the cats, Felix the cat. But my brother was a particularly gifted musician, pianist. And-- and he was also very much interested in science.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born on 26 of November, 1920, in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he lived until?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lived until, I think, 1979. And he die in Fort Worth, Texas, as the Chief Coroner and head of that Institute of Pathology in Fort Worth, Texas.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, just the-- by way of interjection, he was saved at Dachau because of a?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was-- first, during the war, even before the war, the last year of war when he was 17, and he finished high school. He went to the Cadet School in Lemberg. And then in September, instead of coming home, he went with everybody to-- you know, to the war. And then he was a German POW. And then he run away from this POW. And if he didn't, he probably would be much more safe than instead when he came back, because then he was in ghetto with us. Then he was in Plaszow. Then he was in Dachau. And when he went to Dachau, from where-- because before they were sent to Dachau, they were sent to Mauthausen. It was already 19-- 1945. It was maybe March, and they were dragged all around and around. And he was such a skeleton that when they came to registration, there was a Polish priest, who-- also an inmate, who was registering them. And when he noticed such a young guy like my brother and his friend, he right away told them, I give you two minutes to find a Polish name. And so my brother become, from Nelken, [INAUDIBLE], and that other become [INAUDIBLE] from [? Oris. ?] And this saved their life because, unfortunately, the rest of that whole transport went straight to death. And they survive by pretty, a wonder, miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of your daily life in Krakow, could you describe a day in the life in Krakow and the-- perhaps the kind of friends you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, boy. [SIGHS] Well, first of all, the day began with [INAUDIBLE] my nanny, who was up till [INAUDIBLE] and was trying to make me eat my breakfast, which I didn't, and I didn't want it. And then, well, I went to school, and then I came back home. At 1 o'clock, we had a beautiful dinner all together. My brother was eating everything in sight, but I couldn't. And then I was making homework, and nobody was complaining about homework. Maybe we didn't like it very much. But as a matter of fact, I like it. And then I went for a walk with my nanny or with my mother or this. And then parents went to see their friends in cafe or this or that. They were, of course, always at noontime with us and into evening when they did not go somewhere else. But we were playing. I had-- So the house was full of my brother's colleagues. They were Jewish, and they were Polish. And nobody made big deal out of it, well-- I had also many Polish friends, and we were playing. I was playing with dolls quite late. Not like-- I was over 10 years old, and I still have at least six dolls. But I didn't play like a little girl. I was telling them stories, which I promised myself to write, because I knew that I am going to be a writer. So this is how I play. And then they were the friends of my brother, who suddenly took all my dolls and hang them on the chandeliers in the dining room. And I hated this, and throw them out. But, anyway, we were going for-- for walk. We were playing sport. We were going skating and-- and going for ballet dances. Every girl-- I was-- since I was six, I was playing piano. And-- and so it was a quiet life. I knew-- I knew what is expected from me, and there was no reason to revolt. And maybe almost everyone was like this. We hardly-- well, first of all, I hardly become a teenager in a normal time. But it was not this kind of a revolt, rejecting everything and everyone, like it is here, I'm sorry to say. So it was a quiet, warm, very, very cordial life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish community like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we belong, of course, we belong-- I cannot even say whether we belong or not because I don't know this. But I know that we were attending-- it was called temple, it still is, very large synagogue for-- It was like the Reform synagogue here. And there my parents went for the high holidays, for Rosh Hashana, Yom Kippur. And the Passover Seder was very, very-- well, at home, celebrated with my brother asking questions. He, of course, had bar mitzvah. And I wanted to have it too, but no girls get. And-- and even when I was 13, there was already-- nobody was even thinking about this because it was just before the war. So-- well, but I know that there was a Jewish tutor, but I never was there. I know that, because we remembered the songs from that film when parents went [NON-ENGLISH], the Jewish film I saw. But if it was-- I know that some of the girls from my school, which was a state school-- every school was state school, as a matter of fact, because it was the other way like it is here. Private schools did not have these kind of privileges as state school had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's-- let's move into the school in one second.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: If we could just backtrack one moment and let's just close this one other section--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --if we could. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Back in terms of-- of your life as a Jew, did your family celebrate the Sabbath, for instance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not every Sabbath. But particularly in winter, well, I know that my mother like to go to that temple because there was beautiful choir, and she was lighting candles on Friday. On-- but not-- not on a regular basis, but most of the time she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but Seder was very, very much celebrated. And my brother ask always, why is this night different than all the other night? And we had matzos. But, however, once my brother with his colleagues were eating matzos with ham, and this made grandmother very angry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So essentially, you would consider yourself to have been a passionate celebrator of holidays-- but-- but-- but--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- well--

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- but-- but less in the observant category?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- we-- I knew very little about it. We knew very little about it. And well, even in-- in school, we had a religious history of the Jews. But somehow what I remembered from these things, even if there was such a big book about the history of Jews, it was the word 'Sanhedrin.' And I knew much more about the Punic Wars and about-- about [INAUDIBLE] Greece and so forth than about Jewish wars and-- and-- and the other things. However, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I never feel-- had that feeling of being persecuted as a Jewish girl or being resented, except later on, just a year before the war, when I was in a state gymnasium, which was very difficult to get there. And there was an exam and so forth. So I-- we were under five. There were about 50 girls in that class, and we were under five. And there, one day, there was something strange happen, that when we got all new coats, three of these Jewish girls had a Star of David cut out of their coat. And this was such a terrible thing, that to my friend and me, who did not have that coat cut, it was even more hurtful than to other, our friends, who did. And my father, who was very active in the PTA, as you would call it here, went right away to gymnasium, to the principal, and said, I am taking my daughter out of here, and she will go to a private school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this director was very furious. She said, why? This is not-- she did not suffer. My father said, no, I don't want them-- I don't want her to stay here one minute longer. And it's strange. After the war, I attended the same gymnasium. And I went and talked to the same principal, and she asked about my father. And she said, he was such a wonderful man, so handsome, and so useful for PTA. And he took you away. And then said, well, how is he? I said, well, he was-- So this was the first things what happen. Could you tell us briefly, because we're approaching the end of this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --tape, just the name of that school you began-- the name of the school and-- and the type.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was Gymnasium Humanistik, Gymnasium of Adam Mickiewicz in Krakow. And I returned to this gymnasium after the war. And this was strange because I was already after six years of war and three years in concentration camp. And when I saw this little principal, Madame, I was as frightened of her as I was before the war when I was a little student. Well, [INAUDIBLE]. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] why I remember mostly, not so much what they did to us with that cutting our coat, but the feeling that my parents will always protect me. That as long as I am with them, I have nothing to worry. They will never allow anything wrong to happen to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell the name of the school, the state school? You had stated it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Gymnasium--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no, no. The-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mickiewicz. The--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mickiewicz, yeah. Adam Mickiewicz, A-D-A-M, Mickiewicz, M-I-C-K-I-E-W-I-C-Z, Mickiewicz. He was the greatest Polish poet, national Polish poet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the elementary school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was Dabruwki, D-A-B-R-U-W-K-I. Strange enough, that this Dabruwki was in the part of Krakow where later on was the ghetto. And in the school of Dabruwki was [NON-ENGLISH], was where this-- this office where they sent us to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought it's so strange here. Where were our classes? There are files and they take us to work. Also, the-- between 1937 and 1939, was a separate school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Because, well, first, it was the elementary school, and then you have an exam and pass the exam in order to go to gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, but the second one was? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, one was Kollataj. Well, later on. It was K-O-L-L-A-T-A-J.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And the name of the synagogue that you-- or the name of the rabbi of the synagogue that you attended?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know whether he was the rabbi of the synagogue, but he was the rabbi who was teaching us in school, in the state school. It was the Rabbi Schmelkes, Schmelkes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-C-H-M-E-L-K-E-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.
